ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for crispy baby corn exists. Mapped to broader class 'deep-fried food product' (FOODON_03301710) as the closest defensible ancestor. Baby corn as ingredient is covered by FOODON_03301737 (corn food product).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (corn, processed) used as closest defensible analogue. No specific FoodEx2 entry for battered deep-fried baby corn exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Mapped at processed-vegetable level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this prepared snack food. SNOMED CT coverage for restaurant-style fried vegetable dishes is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant starter serving of crispy baby corn is approximately 150 g, comprising 8–10 battered and deep-fried baby corn pieces finished with a garlic tadka.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Corn, sweet, yellow, raw (FDC ID 169998) combined with USDA FoodData Central — Wheat flour, all-purpose, enriched (FDC ID 169761) and USDA FoodData Central — Oil, vegetable, palm (FDC ID 171413); composite estimation for battered deep-fried baby corn
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for crispy baby corn exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by composite modelling: baby corn (~55% by weight, raw equivalent), wheat/cornflour batter (~25%), and absorbed frying oil (~20%). Baby corn nutritional base derived from USDA FDC ID 169998 (sweet corn, yellow, raw). Batter contribution from USDA FDC ID 169761. Oil absorption estimated at 8–12% of final weight per standard deep-frying literature. Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for this preparation style. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for battered deep-fried baby corn. GI estimated at 65 based on analogous deep-fried battered corn/vegetable fritter entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Corn-based battered fried foods cluster in the medium GI range (60–72). A mid-point of 65 is used as a conservative estimate. GL per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 65 × 42.0 / 100 = 27.3. Available carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 28.0 × 1.5 = 42.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per occasion, as a 150 g serving contributes approximately 465 mg sodium — nearly 20% of the 2,400 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (65) and high glycemic load per serving (27.3)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of 27.3 per standard 150 g serving, crispy baby corn poses a significant postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion should be restricted to 50–75 g and paired with high-fibre or protein-rich foods to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat (13.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) from deep-frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process adds significant total fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional food and limit intake to one small serving (≤100 g) to keep saturated fat contribution below 2.1 g per occasion, consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Baby Corn is a popular Indian restaurant starter and snack dish in which whole baby corn cobs are coated in a seasoned batter of wheat flour, cornflour, and spices, then deep-fried in hot oil until golden brown and crunchy. The dish is finished with a garlic tadka — a tempering of sliced or minced garlic briefly fried in oil until fragrant — which is tossed through the fried corn just before serving, imparting a savoury, aromatic depth. The texture is characteristically crisp on the outside with a tender, slightly sweet baby corn interior. In Singapore, this dish is served as an appetiser or side dish at North Indian restaurants, particularly those offering Punjabi-style cuisine. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving provides approximately 368 kcal, 42 g of carbohydrate, 20 g of fat, and 6 g of protein. The medium glycemic index (~65) and high glycemic load (~27) make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content (~465 mg per serving) is relevant for those monitoring cardiovascular and blood pressure risk. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable, but contains gluten from the wheat flour batter. It is not suitable for those with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy baby corn?
Is crispy baby corn gluten free?
Can diabetics eat crispy baby corn?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for crispy baby corn exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for baby corn, wheat flour, and frying oil. GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable deep-fried battered corn/vegetable fritter entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes are mapped at broader or analogous levels. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry for this exact dish exists. Values should be reviewed if ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo publishes a direct entry for battered deep-fried baby corn. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as this is a plant-based fried food with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as no vitamin-D-contributing ingredients are present; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as this dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement study for this dish has not been identified in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 6. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest defensible analogue at the processed-corn level; a more specific code should be applied if the FoodEx2 catalogue is updated. 7. Serving size of 150 g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant starter portions in Singapore; actual restaurant portions may vary from 120–200 g.
